top_papers.title: Transactional Attention: Semantic Sponsorship for KV-Cache Retention
top_papers.summary: At K=16 tokens (0.4% of a 4K context), every existing KV-cache compression method achieves 0% on credential retrieval. The failure mode is dormant tokens: credentials, API keys, and configuration values that receive near-zero attention but become essential at generation time. Because these tokens lack the statistical signals that eviction policies rely on, no method based on attention scores, reconstruction loss, or learned retention gates retains them. We introduce Transactional Attention (TA), a sponsorship mechanism in which structural anchor patterns (e.g., "key:", "password:") protect adjacent value-bearing tokens from eviction. TA achieves 100% credential retrieval at K=16 where six baselines (H2O, TOVA, SnapKV, StreamingLLM, PyramidKV, DynamicKV) achieve 0%, and sustains 100% accuracy across 200 function-calling trials. TA-Fast, an attention-free variant, reduces memory overhead by 52% and is compatible with SDPA and FlashAttention. TA is orthogonal to existing compression methods and adds less than 1% latency overhead.

ai.one_liner_zh: This paper proposes an anchor-based KV-cache retention rule for dormant value tokens and claims it fixes an extreme-budget failure mode that standard compression methods miss.
ai.summary_zh: This paper targets a specific weakness in KV-cache compression: semantically critical tokens such as credentials or configuration values can receive near-zero attention until they are suddenly needed, so standard eviction signals discard them. The proposed Transactional Attention mechanism uses structural anchor patterns like "key:" or "password:" to sponsor adjacent value tokens, and a TA-Fast variant removes attention dependence while remaining compatible with SDPA and FlashAttention. In the abstract, the method is evaluated at a very small cache budget of K=16 tokens and is reported to reach 100% credential retrieval where six named baselines get 0%, while also maintaining 100% accuracy across 200 function-calling trials. If those results hold beyond the described settings, the main value is a cheap, composable fix for a concrete reliability gap in compressed LLM inference. The biggest missing detail is scope: the abstract does not make clear how broad the workloads, prompt formats, hardware setup, and baseline tuning are, so the generality and fairness still need checking.
ai.background_zh: KV-cache compression is increasingly necessary for long-context inference, but common retention signals track observed salience rather than future usefulness, which creates failure cases for dormant tokens.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks how to preserve low-attention but semantically essential tokens under aggressive cache compression without giving up the latency and memory benefits of eviction.
ai.approach_zh: It introduces a sponsorship mechanism in which structural anchors protect nearby value-bearing tokens from eviction, plus an attention-free TA-Fast variant designed to compose with standard attention backends.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract reports 100% credential retrieval at K=16 versus 0% for six baselines, 100% accuracy on 200 function-calling trials, 52% lower memory overhead for TA-Fast, and less than 1% latency overhead, but it does not make the broader evaluation setup clear.
ai.value_zh: If the retention rule generalizes, it could provide a low-overhead way to close an important correctness and safety gap in KV-cache compression while remaining compatible with existing serving kernels and stacks.
open_questions: How diverse are the prompts and anchor formats beyond explicit patterns like "key:" and "password:", and how brittle is the method to formatting variation?
What hardware, model sizes, context lengths, and cache-budget ranges were used, and were the six baselines tuned fairly for the same operating point?
Does the sponsorship rule help on broader long-context retrieval and tool-use workloads, or is the gain concentrated on credential-style key-value patterns?
core_contributions: Identifies dormant value tokens as a concrete failure mode for KV-cache compression under extreme retention budgets.
Introduces structural-anchor sponsorship to retain adjacent semantically important tokens that attention-based policies miss.
Presents an attention-free TA-Fast variant positioned as lower-overhead and compatible with SDPA and FlashAttention.
why_read: It addresses an inference-system correctness issue rather than only average compression quality, which is highly relevant for production serving.
The mechanism is simple and orthogonal to existing KV-cache policies, so it may be easy to reason about and integrate.
The abstract gives unusually sharp failure-mode numbers at an extreme budget, making it worth checking whether the result survives broader evaluation.
risks: The method may depend heavily on explicit textual anchor patterns and fail when important values are not locally marked.
The reported wins may be narrow to credential retrieval and similar function-calling templates rather than general long-context reasoning workloads.
recommended_for: Researchers studying KV-cache eviction and long-context inference reliability.
Engineers building LLM serving systems with aggressive cache compression.
Readers interested in lightweight runtime heuristics that compose with existing attention kernels.
ai.reading_priority_reason: The paper is tightly aligned with LLM inference systems and cache-efficiency work, and it claims a concrete fix for a sharp failure mode with low latency cost; the main reason to read is to verify generality and baseline fairness.
ai.fallback_summary: At K=16 tokens (0.4% of a 4K context), every existing KV-cache compression method achieves 0% on credential retrieval.

top_papers.title: Flow-Controlled Scheduling for LLM Inference with Provable Stability Guarantees
top_papers.summary: Large language models (LLMs) have been widely adopted due to their great performance across a wide range of applications. ChatGPT and Gemini now serve hundreds of millions of active users and handle billions of user requests per day, which puts optimizing LLM inference into the spotlight. A key challenge in LLM inference is that decode lengths are unknown. The memory usage for each request grows with generated tokens, which may lead to overflow and cause system instability. To address this concern, we propose a simple flow-control framework that controls the rate at which prompts join the active set. We derive a necessary condition that any stable system must satisfy and establish sufficient conditions under which our algorithm provably achieves stability. Experiments show that, compared to commonly used strategies in practice, our approach achieves higher token and request throughput, lower average and tail latency, and more stable KV cache utilization.

ai.one_liner_zh: This paper studies admission control for LLM inference and proposes a flow-control scheduler that limits how prompts enter the active set so systems with unknown decode lengths can remain stable under KV-cache growth.
ai.summary_zh: This paper targets a core serving problem in LLM inference: decode lengths are unknown, so per-request KV-cache demand grows during generation and can push the system into overflow or unstable behavior. The proposed method is a simple flow-control framework that regulates the rate at which new prompts join the active set, framing scheduling as a stability problem rather than only a throughput optimization problem. The abstract claims both theory and practice: it derives a necessary condition for any stable system, gives sufficient conditions under which the proposed algorithm is provably stable, and reports experiments against common practical strategies. Those experiments are said to show better token throughput, request throughput, average and tail latency, and more stable KV-cache utilization. The main missing detail is how the controller is instantiated and evaluated in real serving setups, including workload assumptions, baselines, and the tradeoff between aggressiveness and admission delay.
ai.background_zh: LLM serving is now constrained not just by compute but by dynamic memory pressure, because generated sequence lengths are unknown and KV-cache usage grows online during decoding.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks how to schedule LLM inference requests so the system remains stable despite unpredictable decode lengths and growing memory demand that can otherwise cause overflow.
ai.approach_zh: It introduces a flow-control scheduler that controls the rate at which prompts enter the active set and analyzes this controller with necessary and sufficient stability conditions.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract supports the claim that the method has both a formal stability analysis and experimental comparisons, but it does not make the evaluation setup, baseline details, or quantitative margins clear.
ai.value_zh: If the guarantees and experiments hold under realistic workloads, the method could give serving systems a cleaner way to trade admission rate against KV-cache safety, tail latency, and sustained throughput.
open_questions: What exact queueing or workload model underlies the stability proof, and how well does it match real LLM serving traffic with bursty arrivals and heterogeneous prompts?
What are the practical baselines and controller knobs in the experiments, and are throughput and latency gains preserved under fair memory and batching constraints?
How does the method interact with common serving mechanisms such as continuous batching, prefill/decode disaggregation, and KV-cache eviction or reservation policies?
core_contributions: Frames LLM inference scheduling with unknown decode lengths as a flow-control problem tied directly to system stability.
Derives a necessary condition for stability and sufficient conditions under which the proposed scheduler is provably stable.
Reports experimental gains in throughput, latency, and KV-cache utilization stability relative to commonly used practical strategies.
why_read: It is directly about LLM inference serving under KV-cache pressure, which is a central systems bottleneck.
It combines control logic with provable stability conditions instead of treating scheduling as a purely heuristic tuning problem.
It may clarify when admission control improves tail behavior and utilization in decode-heavy workloads.
risks: The abstract does not specify the serving stack, hardware, or workload distribution, so external validity is unclear.
The theoretical model may simplify real systems enough that the guarantees do not transfer cleanly to production schedulers with multiple interacting policies.
recommended_for: Researchers working on LLM inference scheduling and queueing control.
Engineers building serving systems where KV-cache pressure and tail latency dominate operating limits.
Readers interested in theory-backed admission control rather than purely heuristic batching policies.
ai.reading_priority_reason: This is a strong topical match for LLM inference systems and focuses on scheduling, KV-cache stability, throughput, and latency. The paper appears to offer both formal guarantees and systems-facing evidence, which makes it more relevant than a purely empirical serving tweak, although the abstract leaves important evaluation details unspecified.
ai.fallback_summary: Large language models (LLMs) have been widely adopted due to their great performance across a wide range of applications.

top_papers.title: Quantization Dominates Rank Reduction for KV-Cache Compression
top_papers.summary:
We compare two strategies for compressing the KV cache in transformer inference: rank reduction (discard dimensions) and quantization (keep all dimensions, reduce precision). At matched storage budgets across five models (124M-14B, MHA and GQA), we find that quantization consistently outperforms rank reduction by 4-364 PPL depending on model and compression level. The gap persists even when rank reduction is combined with quantization in hybrid baselines, and it grows with GQA aggressiveness. On LAMBADA, INT4 matches FP16 accuracy (+0.23 PPL on Mistral 7B, +0.58 on GPT-2) while rank-32 at identical storage collapses to 0.4%.
  We trace this gap to a structural asymmetry: under softmax attention routing, removing a dimension can flip which token is attended (a discrete failure), while quantization noise is bounded and typically preserves score ordering. We formalize this via a perturbation result showing projection damage exceeds quantization damage by 3 x 2^(2b) per direction under the softmax Fisher metric. A basis ablation confirms the finding is basis-independent (spread <0.4 PPL), establishing that the advantage comes from preserving dimensions, not from a better coordinate system. Joint K+V INT4 quantization achieves 75% total KV reduction at only +0.18 PPL on Mistral 7B.

ai.one_liner_zh: This paper argues that, at fixed KV-cache storage budgets, low-bit quantization preserves transformer quality much better than rank reduction because it keeps the full attention geometry intact.
ai.summary_zh: KV-cache growth is a central inference bottleneck, so this paper asks whether cache compression should discard dimensions or instead keep all dimensions and lower precision. It compares rank reduction, quantization, and hybrid baselines at matched storage budgets across five transformer models spanning 124M to 14B parameters, including both MHA and GQA settings. The claimed result is that quantization consistently beats rank reduction on perplexity, with especially sharp failures for matched-budget rank reduction on LAMBADA, while joint K+V INT4 quantization still gives large cache savings with only a small reported perplexity increase on Mistral 7B. The proposed reason is structural: removing dimensions can flip which token attention selects, whereas quantization noise is bounded and usually preserves score ordering, and the basis ablation is presented as evidence that this is not just a coordinate-choice artifact. The main missing detail is systems-side evidence: the abstract does not say how these methods compare in latency, bandwidth, kernel complexity, or deployment overhead on real hardware.
ai.background_zh: KV-cache memory grows with sequence length and batch size, so compressing keys and values without breaking attention quality is a core problem in long-context LLM inference.
ai.problem_zh: The paper studies whether KV-cache compression should spend its budget on fewer dimensions or on lower precision, and why rank reduction may fail more catastrophically than quantization.
ai.approach_zh: It performs matched-budget comparisons between rank reduction, quantization, and hybrid schemes, then supports the observed gap with a softmax-Fisher perturbation argument and a basis-independence ablation.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract reports results on five models from 124M to 14B with both MHA and GQA, large perplexity gaps favoring quantization, severe matched-budget rank-reduction failure on LAMBADA, and a joint K+V INT4 result on Mistral 7B; it does not make runtime or hardware costs clear.
ai.value_zh: If the result generalizes, it strongly favors full-dimension low-bit KV storage over projection-based compression for practical LLM serving systems.
open_questions: How were matched storage budgets defined in practice, including quantization scales, metadata, and packing overhead versus projection parameters?
What are the actual latency, bandwidth, and kernel-cost tradeoffs of INT4 K+V quantization compared with rank-reduced caches on real serving hardware?
Do the conclusions hold at longer context lengths, on more downstream tasks than perplexity and LAMBADA, and across additional model families?
core_contributions: A matched-budget empirical comparison showing that KV-cache quantization outperforms rank reduction across multiple transformer sizes and attention variants.
A structural explanation that dimension removal can change attention routing discretely, while quantization usually perturbs scores without changing their ordering.
A basis ablation used to argue that the advantage comes from preserving dimensions rather than from a better projection basis.
why_read: It directly addresses a practical KV-cache compression decision for inference systems under equal storage constraints.
It gives a concrete mechanism for why low-bit cache storage may be safer than dimensionality reduction in attention.
It includes GQA behavior, which matters for modern deployment-oriented transformer architectures.
risks: The abstract emphasizes quality outcomes but does not specify latency, throughput, memory-bandwidth, or implementation overhead.
The theoretical result may be locally persuasive, but the abstract does not show how tightly it predicts end-to-end behavior across settings beyond those reported here.
recommended_for: Researchers studying LLM inference efficiency and KV-cache compression.
Engineers choosing between low-bit cache storage and projection-based compression in serving stacks.
Compiler or kernel developers evaluating whether full-dimension INT4 KV paths are better targets than rank-reduced representations.
ai.reading_priority_reason: This is a strong fit for LLM inference systems work because it isolates a deployment-relevant KV-cache design choice, gives concrete matched-budget evidence, and frames the gain in terms of attention behavior rather than generic compression claims.
ai.fallback_summary: We compare two strategies for compressing the KV cache in transformer inference: rank reduction (discard dimensions) and quantization (keep all dimensions, reduce precision).

top_papers.title: EdgeCIM: A Hardware-Software Co-Design for CIM-Based Acceleration of Small Language Models
top_papers.summary: The growing demand for deploying Small Language Models (SLMs) on edge devices, including laptops, smartphones, and embedded platforms, has exposed fundamental inefficiencies in existing accelerators. While GPUs handle prefill workloads efficiently, the autoregressive decoding phase is dominated by GEMV operations that are inherently memory-bound, resulting in poor utilization and prohibitive energy costs at the edge. In this work, we present EdgeCIM, a hardware-software co-design framework that rethinks accelerator design for end-to-end decoder-only inference. At its core is a CIM macro, implemented in 65nm, coupled with a tile-based mapping strategy that balances pipeline stages, maximizing parallelism while alleviating DRAM bandwidth bottlenecks. Our simulator enables design space exploration of SLMs up to 4B parameters, identifying Pareto-optimal configurations in terms of latency and energy. Compared to an NVIDIA Orin Nano, EdgeCIM achieves up to 7.3x higher throughput and 49.59x better energy efficiency on LLaMA3.2-1B, and delivers 9.95x higher throughput than Qualcomm SA8255P on LLaMA3.2-3B. Extensive benchmarks on TinyLLaMA-1.1B, LLaMA3.2 (1B, 3B), Phi-3.5-mini-3.8B, Qwen2.5 (0.5B, 1.5B, 3B), SmolLM2-1.7B, SmolLM3-3B, and Qwen3 (0.6B, 1.7B, 4B) reveal that our accelerator, under INT4 precision, achieves on average 336.42 tokens/s and 173.02 tokens/J. These results establish EdgeCIM as a compelling solution towards real-time, energy-efficient edge-scale SLM inference.

ai.one_liner_zh: A CIM-based edge accelerator co-designed with tile mapping to target memory-bound decode workloads in small language model inference.
ai.summary_zh: This paper targets a real systems bottleneck in edge SLM serving: prefill can be handled reasonably well by GPUs, but autoregressive decode is dominated by memory-bound GEMV and wastes both bandwidth and energy. It proposes EdgeCIM, a hardware-software co-design built around a 65nm compute-in-memory macro plus a tile-based mapping strategy intended to balance pipeline stages and reduce DRAM pressure for end-to-end decoder-only inference. The abstract reports simulator-driven design-space exploration up to 4B-parameter models and claims substantial throughput and energy gains over NVIDIA Orin Nano and Qualcomm SA8255P, with additional averages across multiple SLM families under INT4. If these comparisons are methodologically fair, the practical value is a stronger edge inference path for real-time low-power decoding. The main missing detail is whether the reported gains come from a realistic end-to-end setup with comparable quantization, batching, memory capacity, and prefill/decode accounting rather than from favorable modeling assumptions.
ai.background_zh: Edge deployment of small language models is constrained less by raw compute than by memory-bound decoding, which makes conventional accelerators inefficient on latency and energy.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks how to redesign the accelerator stack for decoder-only SLM inference so that decode-phase GEMV bottlenecks do not dominate edge throughput and power cost.
ai.approach_zh: It combines a 65nm CIM macro with a tile-based software mapping strategy that aims to balance pipeline stages, expose parallelism, and reduce DRAM bandwidth pressure across end-to-end inference.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract provides concrete claimed benchmark results from a simulator and named hardware comparisons across several SLMs under INT4, but it does not make the evaluation methodology or baseline normalization clear.
ai.value_zh: If the setup is sound, the work could matter for edge LLM serving by showing that decode-centric hardware-software co-design can outperform general-purpose edge accelerators on tokens per second and tokens per joule.
open_questions: Are the Orin Nano and SA8255P baselines matched on precision, batching, sequence lengths, and end-to-end prefill/decode accounting?
How much of the gain comes from the CIM macro itself versus the tile mapping and pipeline-balancing strategy?
What memory-capacity, model-placement, and accuracy tradeoffs are required to sustain the reported INT4 results up to 4B parameters?
core_contributions: Frames edge SLM inference around the decode-phase GEMV bottleneck rather than treating prefill-dominant behavior as representative.
Presents a CIM-based accelerator design paired with tile mapping for end-to-end decoder-only inference.
Reports multi-model design-space exploration and hardware comparisons focused on throughput and energy efficiency at the edge.
why_read: Useful if you care about where edge LLM latency and energy actually come from during decode rather than prefill.
Potentially relevant for hardware-runtime co-design decisions around memory bandwidth and model tiling.
Provides a concrete case study of CIM as a decode-oriented inference accelerator for 1B-4B class SLMs.
risks: The main results appear to rely on simulation, so implementation realism and system overheads need scrutiny.

Baseline fairness is unclear from the abstract, especially around INT4, workload configuration, and end-to-end accounting conventions for competitor hardware.` `Hardware specificity may limit portability: a 65nm CIM design optimized for edge decode may not generalize across process nodes, models, or mixed workloads.
recommended_for: Researchers working on LLM inference acceleration and edge serving.
Engineers evaluating decode-heavy hardware-software co-design for small language models.
Readers interested in memory-bandwidth bottlenecks, quantized inference, and alternative accelerator architectures.
ai.reading_priority_reason: Strong fit with LLM inference systems and accelerator co-design, with concrete throughput and energy claims on decoder-heavy edge workloads, though the full read should verify simulator assumptions and baseline fairness.
ai.fallback_summary: The growing demand for deploying Small Language Models (SLMs) on edge devices, including laptops, smartphones, and embedded platforms, has exposed fundamental inefficiencies in existing accelerators.

top_papers.title: Record-Remix-Replay: Hierarchical GPU Kernel Optimization using Evolutionary Search
top_papers.summary:
As high-performance computing and AI workloads become increasingly dependent on GPUs, maintaining high performance across rapidly evolving hardware generations has become a major challenge. Developers often spend months tuning scientific applications to fully exploit new architectures, navigating a complex optimization space that spans algorithm design, source implementation, compiler flags and pass sequences, and kernel launch parameters. Existing approaches can effectively search parts of this space in isolation, such as launch configurations or compiler settings, but optimizing across the full space still requires substantial human expertise and iterative manual effort.
  In this paper, we present Record-Remix-Replay (R^3), a hierarchical optimization framework that combines LLM-driven evolutionary search, Bayesian optimization, and record-replay compilation techniques to efficiently explore GPU kernel optimizations from source-level implementation choices down to compiler pass ordering and runtime configuration. By making candidate evaluation fast and scalable, our approach enables practical end-to-end search over optimization dimensions that are typically treated separately. We show that Record-Remix-Replay can optimize full scientific applications better than traditional approaches over kernel parameters and compiler flags, while also being nearly an order of magnitude faster than modern evolutionary search approaches.
top_papers.matched_domain: LLM Code Generation For CUDA Kernels

match_details.domain: LLM Code Generation For CUDA Kernels

ai.one_liner_zh: This paper presents a hierarchical GPU kernel optimization framework that combines LLM-guided evolutionary search, Bayesian optimization, and record-replay compilation to search source, compiler, and runtime choices together.
ai.summary_zh: The paper targets a real GPU systems problem: performance tuning now spans source-level implementation decisions, compiler pass sequences and flags, and launch-time parameters, and that cross-layer space is too large for manual tuning. It proposes Record-Remix-Replay (R^3), a hierarchical framework that combines LLM-driven evolutionary search with Bayesian optimization and record-replay compilation so candidate variants can be evaluated quickly enough for end-to-end search. The abstract claims this lets the system optimize full scientific applications better than approaches that only tune kernel parameters or compiler flags, while being nearly an order of magnitude faster than modern evolutionary search methods. For systems readers, the main interest is the co-design across program representation, compiler search, and fast evaluation infrastructure rather than any single search heuristic. The biggest missing detail is that the abstract does not make the evaluation setup, hardware targets, benchmark coverage, or baseline fairness clear.
ai.background_zh: GPU performance tuning increasingly depends on coordinated choices across code structure, compiler behavior, and runtime configuration, and new hardware generations make that space harder to maintain manually.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks how to search the full GPU optimization stack jointly, instead of optimizing isolated knobs such as launch parameters or compiler flags, without prohibitive evaluation cost.
ai.approach_zh: R^3 uses a hierarchical search loop that mixes LLM-driven evolutionary proposal generation, Bayesian optimization for efficient exploration, and record-replay compilation to accelerate candidate evaluation across source, compiler, and runtime dimensions.
ai.evidence_zh: According to the abstract, the method improves optimization results for full scientific applications relative to traditional kernel-parameter and compiler-flag tuning approaches, and is nearly an order of magnitude faster than modern evolutionary search methods, but the abstract does not make the experimental setup clear.
ai.value_zh: If the claims hold, this could make cross-layer GPU tuning substantially more practical and reduce the human effort needed to retarget performance-critical workloads to new architectures.
open_questions: What exactly is recorded and replayed, and how much of the reported speedup comes from cheaper evaluation versus better search decisions?
How broad and fair are the baselines across source-level search, compiler pass ordering, and runtime tuning, and are they compared under the same evaluation budget?
How well does the method generalize across GPU generations, application domains, and kernels outside the scientific workloads named in the abstract?
core_contributions: A hierarchical optimization framework that searches source implementation choices, compiler passes and flags, and runtime configuration jointly.
A combination of LLM-driven evolutionary search and Bayesian optimization for navigating a large GPU optimization space.
Use of record-replay compilation to make large-scale candidate evaluation fast enough for practical end-to-end search.
why_read: It treats GPU kernel optimization as a full-stack search problem rather than a single-layer autotuning problem.
The record-replay component may be the main systems contribution if it materially reduces search cost.
It is relevant to compiler/runtime co-design and to any work on LLM-assisted low-level code optimization.
risks: The abstract does not say whether the gains come from stronger search, faster evaluation, or both, which affects how reusable the idea is.
The workload emphasis is scientific applications, so relevance to LLM inference kernels is indirect until kernel types and hardware are shown clearly in the full paper.
recommended_for: Researchers working on GPU autotuning and compiler optimization.
Engineers interested in search over compiler pass ordering and launch/runtime parameters.
Readers studying LLM-assisted low-level code generation or optimization systems.
ai.reading_priority_reason: This is a strong thematic match for GPU kernel optimization and compiler/runtime co-design, with clear relevance to low-level code generation workflows, even though the abstract is centered on scientific applications rather than LLM inference specifically.
ai.fallback_summary: As high-performance computing and AI workloads become increasingly dependent on GPUs, maintaining high performance across rapidly evolving hardware generations has become a major challenge.

daily_brief.overview_zh: Today's main threads are training and inference efficiency, LLM Code Generation For CUDA Kernels. The set is anchored by Transactional Attention: Semantic… and Flow-Controlled Scheduling for LLM…. Quantization Dominates Rank… adds another data point on whether the same line of work carries stronger evidence or engineering value.
top_themes: LLM Code Generation For CUDA Kernels
reading_strategy: Start with Transactional Attention: Semantic… and verify whether it defines the training and inference efficiency problem clearly and exposes a real mechanism.
Then read Flow-Controlled Scheduling for LLM… to see whether the novelty comes from model design, training procedure, or evaluation reframing.
Use Quantization Dominates Rank… as a final cross-check on experimental boundaries and practical value.
